explicit_text: I grew up in Seattle, where my family settled after my father returned from working on the railroad in Nevada. Later in life, I moved back to Seattle after my divorce from Thomas Chang and before marrying Charlie Chew. Seattle was a significant place in my early life and also where I returned to build my family and career.
implicit_text: I grew up in the largest city in Washington state, where my family settled after my father returned from working on the railroad in Nevada. explicit_text: My father emigrated from China to the United States in 1860, and he brought my mother from China in 1890 under false pretenses. I traveled to China several times, first as a little girl and later between 1979 and 1992, witnessing the severe changes brought by the Japanese invasion and the Chinese Communist Revolution. Despite the hardships, the Chinese community in Reno, which I was part of, maintained close ties to our homeland and relatives there.

Despite the hardships, the community from that country in Reno, which I was part of, maintained close ties to our homeland and relatives there.

explicit_text: My father first settled in Port Townsend, Washington after emigrating from China in 1860. It was there that he began his life in America before moving to Nevada to work on the railroad and eventually returning to Washington to become a merchant and community leader. Port Townsend was the starting point of my family's American journey.

explicit_text: Nevada was a challenging place for me, especially in Reno where my husband Charlie Chew and I opened a casino for blacks and Indians who were excluded elsewhere. I experienced discrimination firsthand in Nevada, with people of my race being refused service and housing. Despite these challenges, I ran the Cosmo Club in downtown Reno until 1966 and was deeply involved in the Chinese community there.

explicit_text: I lived in Vancouver, British Columbia with my first husband, Thomas Chang, and we had two children there. Vancouver was an important place during my early married life before I divorced and moved back to Seattle. It was a chapter of my life connected to family and raising children.

explicit_text: Charlie Chew was my second husband, and together we moved to Reno in 1956 where we opened a casino catering to blacks and Indians who were excluded elsewhere. After he died suddenly in 1961, I took over running the club myself until 1966. Charlie was my partner in business and life during a pivotal time.

explicit_text: Reno was where Charlie Chew and I opened the Cosmo Club in 1956, a casino that welcomed blacks and Indians who were otherwise barred from gambling venues. I experienced discrimination there but also became deeply involved in the local Chinese community and ran the club until 1966. Reno was a challenging yet defining place in my life.

I experienced discrimination there but also became deeply involved in the local Chinese community and ran the club until 1966. That Nevada city was a challenging yet defining place in my life.

explicit_text: The Cosmo Club on Commercial Row in downtown Reno was a lively and colorful place known for the popularity of keno, a game introduced by the Chinese. My husband Charlie and I opened it in 1956 to serve blacks and Indians excluded elsewhere, and after his death, I ran it until 1966. The club attracted a diverse and interesting crowd despite the dark side of downtown Reno.

explicit_text: Keno was a game introduced by the Chinese to the American gambling scene and became wildly popular at the Cosmo Club in Reno. It was one of the defining features of the club and helped attract many patrons. I saw firsthand how this game connected cultural traditions with the local gambling culture.

explicit_text: The Joss House Society was a vital organization in the Chinese community in Reno, helping to settle disputes, provide for those in need, and arrange burials. I often served as an interpreter for Chinese people in legal and medical matters there. The society helped maintain our community's cohesion and ties to China.

explicit_text: The Civil Rights Act of 1964 was a turning point for my business because it made discrimination in clubs and restaurants illegal. After its passage, blacks began moving uptown to gamble, which caused business at the Cosmo Club to decline, leading me to close it in 1966. This law changed the social landscape in Reno and affected my livelihood.

explicit_text: After closing the Cosmo Club, I worked with my friend Tom Wong to help run the Sagebrush Club in Fallon. Tom was a trusted colleague, and together we managed a club with a very different clientele, including cowboys, ranchers, and airmen from Fallon Naval Air Station. Our partnership was important in the next chapter of my career.

explicit_text: The Sagebrush Club in Fallon was where I worked with Tom Wong after closing the Cosmo Club. The clientele was quite different from Reno's, mostly cowboys, local business people, ranchers, farmers, and young airmen from Fallon Naval Air Station. It was a new environment and experience for me in the gambling business.

explicit_text: I witnessed the effects of the Japanese invasion of China during my travels back to my ancestral homeland between 1979 and 1992. The invasion had caused severe changes and hardships in the villages of my relatives. Seeing these impacts firsthand was a sobering experience that connected me deeply to my family's history.

explicit_text: The Chinese Communist Revolution was another major event that changed life in China dramatically, which I observed during my visits in later years. The revolution brought severe transformations to my ancestors' village and the lives of relatives. These changes were a stark contrast to the China I had known in my youth.
